justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-7 actually covers 7 rules, splitting cancellation into patient and admin sides). All regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6, note locking BR-7) correctly marked invariant: true. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the minimum of 4, covering booking, double-booking, cancellation window (both patient and admin), unauthorized record access, and note locking. Role-based cancellation rule correctly captured with explicit patient vs. staff administrator distinction. Confidence at 90 is slightly above the ideal 65-90 range ceiling but assumptions are thoroughly stated, which partially compensates.

observations.text: Exceptional depth: 7 business rules instead of the minimum 6, all with correct invariant classification and clear testable assertions
12 acceptance criteria in proper Given/When/Then format, covering all required scenarios plus edge cases like concurrent booking race conditions and admin record access denial
Role-based cancellation boundary explicitly captured with separate rules for patient (BR-3) and admin (BR-4)
Regulatory vs. operational distinction explicitly called out in the Critical Observations section, mapping each rule to its legal/compliance basis
Thorough edge cases section covering license revocation, boundary conditions, timezone issues, and cancelled appointment note locking
Assumptions clearly enumerated including the interpretation of 'treating physician' scope and time interval model
Confidence at 90 is at the upper boundary — given the noted ambiguity about treating physician access scope and license revocation handling, 80-85 would be more defensible
Business impact and stakeholder analysis adds valuable context beyond what was required, correctly identifying HIPAA penalties and the separation of scheduling vs. clinical access for admins

# Domain Analysis — Telemedicine Appointment & Records Platform

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context
This encodes the core scheduling, access control, and clinical documentation lifecycle for a telemedicine platform. The business process spans: physician onboarding (license verification), appointment booking and cancellation, medical record access control, and consultation note immutability. These rules exist at the intersection of operational efficiency, patient safety, and regulatory
